--- SOURCE 1: Space medicine - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_medicine

SUMMARY:
Space Medicine is a subspecialty of Emergency Medicine (Fellowship Training Pathway) which evolved from the Aerospace Medicine specialty . Space Medicine is dedicated to the prevention and treatment of medical conditions that would limit success in space operations.

FULL CONTENT:
![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)
![The Free Encyclopedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-tagline-en.svg)

## Contents

# Space medicine

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/NASA_Medical_Monitoring_2.jpg/250px-NASA_Medical_Monitoring_2.jpg)

**Space Medicine** is a subspecialty of Emergency Medicine (Fellowship Training Pathway) which evolved from the [Aerospace Medicine](/wiki/Aerospace_Medicine "Aerospace Medicine") specialty. Space Medicine is dedicated to the prevention and treatment of medical conditions that would limit success in space operations. Space medicine focuses specifically on prevention, acute care, emergency medicine, wilderness medicine, hyper/hypobaric medicine in order to provide medical care of [astronauts](/wiki/Astronaut "Astronaut") and [spaceflight participants](/wiki/Spaceflight_participant "Spaceflight participant"). The spaceflight environment poses many unique stressors to the human body, including [G forces](/wiki/G-force "G-force"), [microgravity](/wiki/Micro-g_environment "Micro-g environment"), unusual atmospheres such as low pressure or high carbon dioxide, and [space radiation](/wiki/Health_threat_from_cosmic_rays "Health threat from cosmic rays"). Space medicine applies space physiology, preventive medicine, primary care, emergency medicine, acute care medicine, austere medicine, public health, and toxicology to prevent and treat medical problems in space. This expertise is additionally used to inform vehicle systems design to minimize the risk to human health and performance while meeting mission objectives.

[Astronautical hygiene](/wiki/Astronautical_hygiene "Astronautical hygiene") is the application of science and technology to the prevention or control of exposure to the hazards that may cause astronaut ill health. Both these sciences work together to ensure that astronauts work in a safe environment. Medical consequences such as possible [visual impairment](/wiki/Blindness "Blindness") and [bone loss](/wiki/Osteoporosis "Osteoporosis") have been associated with [human spaceflight](/wiki/Human_spaceflight "Human spaceflight").[[2]](#cite_note-NYT-20140127-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Wired-20120723-3)

In October 2015, the [NASA Office of Inspector General](/wiki/NASA_Office_of_Inspector_General "NASA Office of Inspector General") issued a [health hazards report](/wiki/Effect_of_spaceflight_on_the_human_body "Effect of spaceflight on the human body") related to [space exploration](/wiki/Space_exploration "Space exploration"), including a [human mission to Mars](/wiki/Human_mission_to_Mars "Human mission to Mars").[[4]](#cite_note-AP-20151029-4)[[5]](#cite_note-NASA-20151029oig-5)

## History

[Hubertus Strughold](/wiki/Hubertus_Strughold "Hubertus Strughold") (1898–1987), a former [Nazi](/wiki/Nazi "Nazi") physician and physiologist, was brought to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") as part of [Operation Paperclip](/wiki/Operation_Paperclip "Operation Paperclip").[[6]](#cite_note-6) He first coined the term "space medicine" in 1948 and was the first and only Professor of Space Medicine at the [School of Aviation Medicine](/wiki/School_of_Aviation_Medicine "School of Aviation Medicine") (SAM) at [Randolph Air Force Base](/wiki/Randolph_Air_Force_Base "Randolph Air Force Base"), [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"). In 1949, Strughold was made director of the [Department of Space Medicine](/w/index.php?title=Department_of_Space_Medicine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Department of Space Medicine (page does not exist)") at the SAM (which is now the [US Air Force School of Aerospace Medicine](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force_School_of_Aerospace_Medicine "United States Air Force School of Aerospace Medicine") (USAFSAM) at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, Ohio. He played an important role in developing the [pressure suit](/wiki/Pressure_suit "Pressure suit") worn by early American astronauts. He was a co-founder of the Space Medicine Branch of the [Aerospace Medical Association](/wiki/Aerospace_Medical_Association "Aerospace Medical Association") in 1950. The aeromedical library at Brooks AFB was named after him in 1977, but later renamed because documents from the [Nuremberg War Crimes Tribunal](/wiki/Nuremberg_Trials "Nuremberg Trials") linked Strughold to medical experiments in which inmates of the [Dachau concentration camp](/wiki/Dachau_concentration_camp "Dachau concentration camp") were tortured and killed.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Soviet research into Space Medicine was centered at the Scientific Research Testing Institute of Aviation Medicine (NIIAM). In 1949, A.M. Vasilevsky, the Minister of Defense of the USSR, gave instructions via the initiative of [Sergei Korolev](/wiki/Sergei_Korolev "Sergei Korolev") to NIIAM to conduct biological and medical research. In 1951, NIIAM began to work on the first research work entitled "Physiological and hygienic substantiation of flight capabilities in special conditions", which formulated the main research tasks, the necessary requirements for pressurized cabins, life support systems, rescue and control and recording equipment. At the Korolev design bureau, they created rockets for lifting animals within 200–250 km and 500–600 km, and then began to talk about developing artificial satellites and launching a man into space.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Then in 1963 the Institute for Biomedical Problems (IMBP) was founded to undertake the study of space medicine.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Animal testing

Before sending humans, space agencies used animals to study the effects of space travel on the body.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) After several years of failed animal recoveries, an [Aerobee](/wiki/Aerobee "Aerobee") rocket launch in September 1951 was the first safe return of a monkey and a group of mice from near space altitudes.[[11]](#cite_note-:12-11) On 3 November 1957, [Sputnik 2](/wiki/Sputnik_2 "Sputnik 2") became the first mission to carry a living animal to space, a dog named [Laika](/wiki/Laika "Laika"). This flight and others suggested the possibility of safely flying in space within a controlled environment, and provided data on how living beings react to space flight.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) Later flights with cameras to observe the animal subjects would show in flight conditions such as high-G and zero-G.[[11]](#cite_note-:12-11) Russian tests yielded more valuable physiological data from the animal tests.[[11]](#cite_note-:12-11)

On January 31, 1961, a chimpanzee named [Ham](/wiki/Ham_(chimpanzee) "Ham (chimpanzee)") was launched into a [sub-orbital](/wiki/Sub-orbital_spaceflight "Sub-orbital spaceflight") flight aboard a [Mercury-Redstone Launch Vehicle](/wiki/Mercury-Redstone_Launch_Vehicle "Mercury-Redstone Launch Vehicle"). The flight was meant to model the planned mission of astronaut [Alan Shepard](/wiki/Alan_Shepard "Alan Shepard"). The mission planned to reach an altitude of 115 miles, and speeds up to 4400 miles per hour.[[12]](#cite_note-Animals_in_Space-12) However, the actual flight reached 157 miles and a maximum speed of 5857 miles per hour.[[12]](#cite_note-Animals_in_Space-12) During flight, Ham experienced 6.6 minutes of [weightlessness](/wiki/Weightlessness "Weightlessness"). After splashing down in the Atlantic Ocean, Ham was recovered by the [USS Donner.](/wiki/USS_Donner_(LSD-20) "USS Donner (LSD-20)")[[13]](#cite_note-13) He suffered only limited injuries during flight, only receiving a bruised nose.[[14]](#cite_note-:02-14) Ham's vital signs were monitored and collected throughout the 16 minute flight, and used to develop [life support systems](/wiki/Life_support "Life support") for later human astronauts.[[14]](#cite_note-:02-14)

Animal testing in space continues currently, with mice, ants, and other animals regularly being sent to the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station").[[15]](#cite_note-:03-15) In 2014, eight ant colonies were sent to the ISS to investigate the group behavior of ants in microgravity. The ISS allows for the investigation of animal behavior without sending them in specifically designed capsules.[[15]](#cite_note-:03-15)

### North American X-15

Rocket-powered aircraft North American X-15 provided an early opportunity to study the effects of a near-space environment on human physiology.[[16]](#cite_note-16) At its highest operational speed and altitude, the X-15 provided approximately five minutes of weightlessness. This opportunity allowed for the development of devices to facilitate working in low pressure, high acceleration environments such as pressure suits, and [telemetering](/wiki/Telemetry "Telemetry") systems to collect physiological data.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17) This data and technologies allowed for better mission planning for future space missions.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17)

### Project Mercury

Space medicine was a critical factor in the United States human space program, starting with [Project Mercury](/wiki/Project_Mercury "Project Mercury").[[18]](#cite_note-Mills-1965-18) The main precaution taken by Mercury astronauts to defend against high G environments like launch and reentry was a couch with seat belts to make sure astronauts were not forcibly moved from their position. Additionally, experienced pilots proved to be better able to cope with high G scenarios.[[11]](#cite_note-:12-11) One of the pressing concerns with Project Mercury's mission environment was the isolated nature of the cabin. There were deeper concerns about psychological issues than there were about physiological health effects. Substantial animal testing proved beyond a reasonable doubt to NASA engineers that spaceflight could be done safely provided a climate controlled environment.[[11]](#cite_note-:12-11)

### Project Gemini

The [Gemini](/wiki/Project_Gemini "Project Gemini") program primarily addressed the psychological issues from isolation in space with two crewmembers. Upon returning from space, it was recorded that crewmembers experienced a loss of balance and a decrease in anaerobic ability.[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19)

### Project Apollo

The [Apollo](/wiki/Apollo_program "Apollo program") program began with a substantial basis of medical knowledge and precautions from both Mercury and [Gemini](/wiki/Project_Gemini "Project Gemini"). The understanding of high and low G environments was well documented and the effects of isolation had been addressed with Gemini and Apollo having multiple occupants in one capsule. The primary research of the Apollo Program focused on pre-flight and post-flight monitoring.[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19) Some Apollo mission plans were postponed or altered due to some or all crewmembers contracting a communicable disease. Apollo 14 instituted a form of quarantine for crewmembers so as to curb the passing of typical illnesses.[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19) While the efficacy of the Flight Crew Health Stabilization Program was questionable as some crewmembers still contracted diseases,[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19) the program showed enough results to maintain implementation with current space programs.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Effects of space-travel

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Space_body_fluid.svg/500px-Space_body_fluid.svg.png)

In October 2018, [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA")-funded researchers found that lengthy journeys into [outer space](/wiki/Outer_space "Outer space"), including travel to the [planet Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars"), may substantially damage the [gastrointestinal tissues](/wiki/Gastrointestinal_tract "Gastrointestinal tract") of astronauts. The studies support earlier work that found such journeys could significantly damage the [brains](/wiki/Brain "Brain") of [astronauts](/wiki/Astronaut "Astronaut"), and age them prematurely.[[21]](#cite_note-TI-20181002-21)

In November 2019, researchers reported that [astronauts](/wiki/Astronaut "Astronaut") experienced serious [blood flow](/wiki/Hemodynamics "Hemodynamics") and [clot](/wiki/Thrombus "Thrombus") problems while on board the International Space Station, based on a six-month study of 11 healthy astronauts. The results may influence long-term [spaceflight](/wiki/Spaceflight "Spaceflight"), including a mission to the planet [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars"), according to the researchers.[[22]](#cite_note-CNN-20191115-22)[[23]](#cite_note-JAMA-20191113-23)

### Blood clots

Deep vein thrombosis of the internal jugular vein of the neck was first discovered in 2020 in an astronaut on a long duration stay on the ISS, requiring treatment with blood thinners.[[24]](#cite_note-24) A subsequent study of eleven astronauts found slowed blood flow in the neck veins and even reversal of blood flow in two of the astronauts.[[25]](#cite_note-25) NASA is currently conducting more research to study whether these abnormalities could predispose astronauts to blood clots.

### Cardiac rhythms

[Heart rhythm disturbances](/wiki/Cardiac_dysrhythmia "Cardiac dysrhythmia") have been seen among astronauts.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Most of these have been related to [cardiovascular disease](/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease"), but it is not clear whether this was due to [pre-existing conditions](/wiki/Pre-existing_condition "Pre-existing condition") or [effects of space flight](/wiki/Effect_of_spaceflight_on_the_human_body "Effect of spaceflight on the human body"). It is hoped that advanced screening for [coronary disease](/wiki/Coronary_disease "Coronary disease") has greatly mitigated this risk. Other heart rhythm problems, such as [atrial fibrillation](/wiki/Atrial_fibrillation "Atrial fibrillation"), can develop over time, necessitating periodic screening of crewmembers’ heart rhythms. Beyond these terrestrial heart risks, some concern exists that prolonged exposure to [microgravity](/wiki/Microgravity "Microgravity") may lead to heart rhythm disturbances. Although this has not been observed to date, further surveillance is warranted.

### Decompression illness in spaceflight

In space, astronauts use a [space suit](/wiki/Space_suit "Space suit"), essentially a self-contained individual spacecraft, to do spacewalks, or [extra-vehicular activities](/wiki/Extra-vehicular_activity "Extra-vehicular activity") (EVAs). Spacesuits are generally inflated with 100% [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") at a total pressure that is less than a third of normal [atmospheric pressure](/wiki/Atmospheric_pressure "Atmospheric pressure"). Eliminating inert atmospheric components such as [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") allows the astronaut to breathe comfortably, but also have the mobility to use their hands, arms, and legs to complete required work, which would be more difficult in a higher pressure suit.

After the astronaut dons the spacesuit, air is replaced by 100% oxygen in a process called a "nitrogen purge". In order to reduce the risk of [decompression sickness](/wiki/Decompression_sickness "Decompression sickness"), the astronaut must spend several hours "pre-breathing" at an intermediate nitrogen [partial pressure](/wiki/Partial_pressure "Partial pressure"), in order to let their body tissues [outgas](/wiki/Outgassing "Outgassing") nitrogen slowly enough that bubbles are not formed. When the astronaut returns to the "shirt sleeve" environment of the spacecraft after an EVA, pressure is restored to whatever the operating pressure of that spacecraft may be, generally normal atmospheric pressure. [Decompression illness](/wiki/Decompression_illness "Decompression illness") in spaceflight consists of decompression sickness (DCS) and other injuries due to uncompensated changes in pressure, or [barotrauma](/wiki/Barotrauma "Barotrauma").

#### Decompression sickness

[Decompression sickness](/wiki/Decompression_sickness "Decompression sickness") is the injury to the tissues of the body resulting from the presence of nitrogen bubbles in the tissues and blood. This occurs due to a rapid reduction in ambient pressure causing the dissolved nitrogen to come out of solution as gas bubbles within the body.[[27]](#cite_note-27) In space the risk of DCS is significantly reduced by using a technique to wash out the nitrogen in the body's tissues. This is achieved by breathing 100% oxygen for a specified period of time before donning the spacesuit, and is continued after a nitrogen purge.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) DCS may result from inadequate or interrupted pre-oxygenation time, or other factors including the astronaut's level of hydration, physical conditioning, prior injuries and age. Other risks of DCS include inadequate nitrogen purge in the EMU, a strenuous or excessively prolonged EVA, or a loss of suit pressure. Non-EVA crewmembers may also be at risk for DCS if there is a loss of spacecraft cabin pressure.

Symptoms of DCS in space may include chest pain, shortness of breath, cough or pain with a deep breath, unusual fatigue, lightheadedness, dizziness, headache, unexplained musculoskeletal pain, tingling or numbness, extremities weakness, or visual abnormalities.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Primary treatment principles consist of in-suit repressurization to re-dissolve nitrogen bubbles,[[31]](#cite_note-31) 100% oxygen to re-oxygenate tissues,[[32]](#cite_note-32) and hydration to improve the circulation to injured tissues.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

#### Barotrauma

[Barotrauma](/wiki/Barotrauma "Barotrauma") is the injury to the tissues of air filled spaces in the body as a result of differences in pressure between the body spaces and the ambient atmospheric pressure. Air filled spaces include the middle ears, paranasal sinuses, lungs and gastrointestinal tract.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-usn-35) One would be predisposed by a pre-existing upper respiratory infection, nasal allergies, recurrent changing pressures, dehydration, or a poor equalizing technique.

Positive pressure in the air filled spaces results from reduced barometric pressure during the depressurization phase of an EVA.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) It can cause abdominal distension, ear or sinus pain, decreased hearing, and dental or jaw pain.[[35]](#cite_note-usn-35)[[38]](#cite_note-38) Abdominal distension can be treated with extending the abdomen, gentle massage and encourage passing [flatus](/wiki/Flatus "Flatus"). Ear and sinus pressure can be relieved with passive release of positive pressure.[[39]](#cite_note-pp-39) Pretreatment for susceptible individuals can include oral and nasal [decongestants](/wiki/Decongestant "Decongestant"), or oral and nasal [steroids](/wiki/Steroid "Steroid").[[40]](#cite_note-med-40)

Negative pressure in air fill spaces results from increased [barometric pressure](/wiki/Barometric_pressure "Barometric pressure") during repressurization after an EVA or following a planned restoration of a reduced cabin pressure. Common symptoms include ear or sinus pain, decreased hearing, and tooth or jaw pain.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Treatment may include active positive pressure equalization of ears and sinuses,[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[39]](#cite_note-pp-39) oral and nasal decongestants, or oral and nasal steroids, and appropriate pain medication if needed.[[40]](#cite_note-med-40)

### Decreased immune system functioning

Astronauts in space have weakened immune systems, which means that in addition to increased vulnerability to new exposures, viruses already present in the body—which would normally be suppressed—become active.[[43]](#cite_note-43) In space, [T-cells](/wiki/T-cells "T-cells") do not reproduce properly, and the cells that do exist are less able to
fight off infection.[[44]](#cite_note-44) NASA research is measuring the change in the immune systems of its astronauts as well as performing experiments with T-cells in space.

On April 29, 2013, scientists in Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, funded by [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA"), reported that, during [spaceflight](/wiki/Spaceflight "Spaceflight") on the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station"), [microbes](/wiki/Microbes "Microbes") seem to adapt to the [space environment](/wiki/Space_environment "Space environment") in ways "not observed on Earth" and in ways that "can lead to increases in growth and [virulence](/wiki/Virulence "Virulence")".[[45]](#cite_note-PLos-20130429-45)

In March 2019, NASA reported that latent [viruses](/wiki/Virus "Virus") in humans may be activated during [space missions](/wiki/Human_spaceflight "Human spaceflight"), adding possibly more risk to astronauts in future deep-space missions.[[46]](#cite_note-EA-20190315-46)

### Increased infection risk

A 2006 Space Shuttle experiment found that *[Salmonella typhimurium](/wiki/Salmonella_typhimurium "Salmonella typhimurium")*, a bacterium that can cause [food poisoning](/wiki/Food_poisoning "Food poisoning"), became more virulent when cultivated in space.[[47]](#cite_note-ASU-20070923-47) On April 29, 2013, scientists in Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, funded by [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA"), reported that, during [spaceflight](/wiki/Spaceflight "Spaceflight") on the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station"), [microbes](/wiki/Microbes "Microbes") seem to adapt to the [space environment](/wiki/Space_environment "Space environment") in ways "not observed on Earth" and in ways that "can lead to increases in growth and [virulence](/wiki/Virulence "Virulence")".[[45]](#cite_note-PLos-20130429-45) More recently, in 2017, [bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") were found to be more resistant to [antibiotics](/wiki/Antibiotic "Antibiotic") and to thrive in the near-weightlessness of space.[[48]](#cite_note-GZM-20170913-48) [Microorganisms](/wiki/Microorganism "Microorganism") have been observed to survive the [vacuum](/wiki/Vacuum "Vacuum") of outer space.[[49]](#cite_note-Dose-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Horneck-50) Researchers in 2018 reported, after detecting the presence on the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station") (ISS) of five *[Enterobacter bugandensis](/wiki/Enterobacter "Enterobacter")* bacterial strains, none [pathogenic](/wiki/Pathogen "Pathogen") to humans, that [microorganisms](/wiki/Microorganism "Microorganism") on ISS should be carefully monitored to continue assuring a medically healthy environment for [astronauts](/wiki/Astronaut "Astronaut").[[51]](#cite_note-EA-20181122-51)[[52]](#cite_note-BMC-20181123-52)

### Effects of fatigue

[Human spaceflight](/wiki/Human_spaceflight "Human spaceflight") often requires astronaut crews to endure long periods without rest. Studies have shown that lack of sleep can cause [fatigue](/wiki/Fatigue_(medical) "Fatigue (medical)") that leads to errors while performing critical tasks.[[53]](#cite_note-Harrison_1998-53)[[54]](#cite_note-Durmer_2005-54)[[55]](#cite_note-Banks_2007-55) Also, individuals who are fatigued often cannot determine the degree of their impairment.[[56]](#cite_note-56)
Astronauts and ground crews frequently suffer from the effects of [sleep deprivation](/wiki/Sleep_deprivation "Sleep deprivation") and [circadian rhythm disruption](/wiki/Circadian_rhythm#Disruption "Circadian rhythm"). Fatigue due to sleep loss, sleep shifting and [work overload](/wiki/Occupational_stress "Occupational stress") could cause performance errors that put space flight participants at risk of compromising mission objectives as well as the health and safety of those on board.

### Loss of balance

Leaving and returning to Earth's gravity causes “space sickness,” dizziness, and loss of balance in astronauts. By studying how changes can affect balance in the human body—involving the senses, the brain, the inner ear, and blood pressure—NASA hopes to develop treatments that can be used on Earth and in space to correct balance disorders. Until then, NASA's astronauts must rely on a medication called [Midodrine](/wiki/Midodrine "Midodrine") (an “anti-dizzy” pill that temporarily increases blood pressure), and/or [promethazine](/wiki/Promethazine "Promethazine") to help carry out the tasks they need to do to return home safely.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### Loss of bone density

[Spaceflight osteopenia](/wiki/Spaceflight_osteopenia "Spaceflight osteopenia") is the [bone loss](/wiki/Bone_Loss "Bone Loss") associated with [human spaceflight](/wiki/Human_spaceflight "Human spaceflight").[[3]](#cite_note-Wired-20120723-3) The metabolism of calcium is limited in microgravity and will cause calcium to leak out of bones.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) After a 3–4 month trip into space, it takes about 2–3 years to regain lost bone density.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) New techniques are being developed to help astronauts recover faster. Research in the following areas holds the potential to aid the process of growing new bone:

### Loss of muscle mass

In space, muscles in the legs, back, spine, and heart weaken and waste away because they no longer are needed to overcome gravity, just as people lose muscle when they age due to reduced physical activity.[[3]](#cite_note-Wired-20120723-3) Astronauts rely on research in the following areas to build muscle and maintain body mass:

### Impairment of eyesight

During long [space flight](/wiki/Space_flight "Space flight") missions, astronauts may develop ocular changes and visual impairment collectively known as the Space Associated Neuro-ocular Syndrome (SANS).[[2]](#cite_note-NYT-20140127-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Wired-20120723-3)[[61]](#cite_note-Mader-2011-61)[[62]](#cite_note-Puiu-20111109-62)[[63]](#cite_note-CNN-20120109-63)[[64]](#cite_note-Space-20120313-64)[[65]](#cite_note-Radiology-20120313-65)[[66]](#cite_note-SKR-20171103-66) Such vision problems may be a major concern for future deep space flight missions, including a [human mission to Mars](/wiki/Human_mission_to_Mars "Human mission to Mars").[[61]](#cite_note-Mader-2011-61)[[62]](#cite_note-Puiu-20111109-62)[[63]](#cite_note-CNN-20120109-63)[[64]](#cite_note-Space-20120313-64)[[67]](#cite_note-WIRED-20140212-67)

### Loss of mental abilities and risk of Alzheimer's disease

On December 31, 2012, a [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA")-supported study reported that [human spaceflight](/wiki/Human_spaceflight "Human spaceflight") may harm the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain") of [astronauts](/wiki/Astronauts "Astronauts") and accelerate the onset of [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease").[[68]](#cite_note-PLOS-20121231-68)[[69]](#cite_note-SpaceRef-20130101-69)[[70]](#cite_note-NasaWatch-20130103-70)

On 2 November 2017, scientists reported that significant changes in the position and structure of the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain") have been found in [astronauts](/wiki/Astronaut "Astronaut") who have taken [trips in space](/wiki/Human_spaceflight "Human spaceflight"), based on [MRI studies](/wiki/Magnetic_resonance_imaging "Magnetic resonance imaging"). Astronauts who took longer space trips were associated with greater brain changes.[[71]](#cite_note-NEJM-20171102-71)[[72]](#cite_note-QTZ-20171103-72)

### Orthostatic intolerance

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Beckman_instruments_incorp_8049g5371.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-Beckman_instruments_incorp_8049g5371.tiff.jpg)

Under the influence of the earth's [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity"), blood and other body fluids are pulled towards the lower body when standing. When gravity is removed during space exploration, hydrostatic pressures throughout the body are removed and the resulting change in blood distribution may be similar lying down on Earth where hydrostatic differences are minimized. Upon return to earth, reduced blood volume from spaceflight results in [orthostatic hypotension](/wiki/Orthostatic_hypotension "Orthostatic hypotension").[[74]](#cite_note-Dizzy-74) Orthostatic tolerance after spaceflight has been greatly improved by fluid loading countermeasures taken by astronauts before landing.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### Radiation effects

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/PIA17601-Comparisons-RadiationExposure-MarsTrip-20131209.png/250px-PIA17601-Comparisons-RadiationExposure-MarsTrip-20131209.png)

Soviet [cosmonaut](/wiki/Cosmonaut "Cosmonaut") [Valentin Lebedev](/wiki/Valentin_Lebedev "Valentin Lebedev"), who spent 211 days in orbit during 1982 (an absolute record for stay in Earth's orbit), lost his eyesight to progressive [cataract](/wiki/Cataract "Cataract"). Lebedev stated: “I suffered from a lot of radiation in space. It was all concealed back then, during the Soviet years, but now I can say that I caused damage to my health because of that flight.”[[3]](#cite_note-Wired-20120723-3)[[80]](#cite_note-80) On 31 May 2013, NASA scientists reported that a possible [human mission to Mars](/wiki/Human_mission_to_Mars "Human mission to Mars") may involve a great [radiation risk](/wiki/Radiation "Radiation") based on the amount of [energetic particle radiation](/wiki/Radiation "Radiation") detected by the [RAD](/wiki/Radiation_assessment_detector "Radiation assessment detector") on the [Mars Science Laboratory](/wiki/Mars_Science_Laboratory "Mars Science Laboratory") while traveling from the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") to [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") in 2011–2012.[[67]](#cite_note-WIRED-20140212-67)[[76]](#cite_note-SCI-20130531a-76)[[77]](#cite_note-SCI-20130531b-77)[[78]](#cite_note-NYT-20130530-78)[[79]](#cite_note-SN-20130629-79)

### Loss of kidney function

On 11 June 2024 researchers at the University College of London's Department of Renal Medicine reported that "Serious health risks emerge (with respect to the kidneys) the longer a person is exposed to Galactic Radiation and microgravity."[[81]](#cite_note-NC-20240611-81)[[82]](#cite_note-YN-20240612-82)[[83]](#cite_no

--- SOURCE 2: SPACE MEDICINE Definition & Meaning - Merriam-Webster ---
URL: https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/space%20medicine

SUMMARY:
The meaning of SPACE MEDICINE is a branch of medicine that deals with the physiological and biological effects on the human body of spaceflight.

FULL CONTENT:
![UCLA Health](/themes/custom/uclahealth/dist/images/ucla-health-logo.svg)

# Space Medicine

## Sub-navigation

## **The UCLA Space Medicine Center**

![Space Medicine Logo](/sites/default/files/styles/max_width_006000_240/public/images/95/space-medicine-logo.png?f=0456a3b1&itok=kuNLW_yi)

The UCLA Space Medicine Center is a hub of innovation, where medicine meets space exploration to create new possibilities for human health and safety beyond Earth. As pioneers in the medical field, we are dedicated to tackling the unique challenges of healthcare in space by developing new medical technologies, treatment methodologies, and training pathways to ensure the health and safety of astronauts as we pursue making our species multiplanetary. 

As a cornerstone in aerospace advancements, UCLA is the leading academic institution for space medicine on the west coast. Collaborations with [JPL](https://www.jpl.nasa.gov/) on space health research, participation in [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/) missions, one of just a handful of academic affiliates of the [US Space Command](https://www.spacecom.mil/),development of biomedical devices for space, and educational initiatives with [SpaceX](https://www.spacex.com/) and [VAST](VAST ) highlight UCLA’s commitment to advancing both aerospace technology and space medicine. Our proactive approach to solving health challenges underscores UCLA's integral role in shaping the future of space medicine. At the UCLA Space Medicine Center, we empower astronauts to reach for the stars with our unparalleled training and treatments, ensuring a safe and successful journey into the future.

## **What is Space Medicine?**

![Nasa doctors](/sites/default/files/styles/landscape_3x2_032000_1280x853/public/images/03/nasa-medical-monitoring-2.jpg?f=32f143b4&itok=yS0E0bSF)

Space Medicine is a specialized field of medicine that focuses on maintaining the health, safety, and performance of crewmembers in the harsh environment of space and distant planetary bodies. Microgravity, radiation exposure, and the psychological stressors of confinement and isolation are some of the hardships astronauts face while traveling through space, where traditional medical procedures and treatments may not be feasible. Through developing new and innovative techniques and treating illnesses in space, our program aims to enhance the survivability and success of astronauts in order to make long-term space habitation possible–both now, and for future generations of space explorers.

## **What The Space Medicine Center Does Today**

Our Space Medicine Center at UCLA continues to lead in cutting-edge research and clinical practice, applying evidence-based medicine to advance human health in space. While we cannot disclose all operational details due to ongoing contractual agreements, our team actively performs medical screening of astronauts, trains crew members in critical medical procedures, supplies novel medications for spaceflight, and conducts specialized research for the private spaceflight industry. We also employ multiple flight surgeons who work across the private space industry, manage in-flight medical care, and oversee pre- and post-mission programs including launch and recovery operations. Additionally, as one of only a few academic affiliates of the United States Space Command, we play a pivotal role in national efforts to ensure safe and effective space operations. In line with our academic mission, our Center delivers comprehensive medical education, preparing the next generation of aerospace clinicians and fostering innovation that benefits both spacefarers and patients on Earth.

## **Training for the New Frontier**

[The UCLA Space Medicine Fellowship](https://emergencymedicine.ucla.edu/education/fellowships/space-medicine), established in 2021, is the first emergency medicine space medicine fellowship program. It is designed to equip physicians with the skills needed to provide healthcare in space environments. The program offers a robust curriculum that includes clinical rotations, aerospace engineering education, and hands-on experiences with space agencies. 

The fellowship includes rotations at [SpaceX](https://www.spacex.com/) and [VAST](https://www.vastspace.com/), specialized engineering coursework developed with [Caltech](https://www.caltech.edu/) and [NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory](https://www.jpl.nasa.gov/), and biomechanical engineering training at [UCLA’s Samueli School of Engineering](https://samueli.ucla.edu/). Fellows also have the ability to undergo austere medical training in antarctica and participate in Mars analog missions to simulate the conditions and health risks on Martian surfaces. We are also developing Allied Health training programs for paramedics and nurses, aiming to expand our training capabilities to support the growing demands of space missions.

## **Our Team**

Our team features key members from all aspects of the public and private aerospace industry. Dr. Haig Aintablian, MD, the director of the UCLA Space Medicine Program, leads this initiative and has an extensive background in emergency medicine and space medicine, having co-founded the nation’s first emergency medicine-based space medicine training pathway with Jo Feldman, MD and Gregory Hendey, MD. Between them, Haig and Jo have supported countless astronaut missions for the public and private industries and have been integral in the advancement of UCLA’s space medicine initiative.

## The Future of the UCLA Space Medicine Center

Our vision is to establish a comprehensive healthcare framework for space missions, ensuring both the immediate health needs of astronauts and the long-term sustainability of human life in space. From developing a Virtual Space Medicine Center to a Lunar and Martian Mohave Desert Analog Site, UCLA Space Medicine Center will combine cutting-edge technology, rigorous training protocols, and ever-expanding innovations to define the new era of space medicine.

![Rendering of the futuristic UCLA Health Space Medicine Center, with doctors, researchers, and space health icons on the glass facade.](/sites/default/files/styles/max_width_016000_640/public/images/1d/thumbnail-image004.png?f=711f6f71&itok=SXnpIbh-)

Our vision is to establish a comprehensive healthcare framework for space missions, ensuring both the immediate health needs of astronauts and the long-term sustainability of human life in space. Below are the key goals and projects that outline our phased approach to achieving this vision:

**Current Phase (0-2 years):**

**Intermediate Phase (3-5 years):**

**Definitive Phase (5-7 years):**

![UCLA Health's Mars analog mission illustration: A futuristic Mars landscape with a person in a spacesuit, symbolizing research and innovation.](/sites/default/files/styles/landscape_3x2_032000_1280x853/public/images/6c/mars-analog.png?h=c5eb578b&f=58df8892&itok=p2_8ZPLW)

By achieving these milestones, we aim to ensure the safety, productivity, and success of humanity’s ventures into space. This vision leverages UCLA's strategic location, existing programs, and relationships with aerospace giants like SpaceX and NASA, positioning us at the forefront of space medicine advancements.

![Dr. Haig Aintablian](/sites/default/files/styles/max_width_016000_640/public/images/1e/thumbnail-image005_0.jpg?f=9cece7ad&itok=hVJGq03_)

## Join Us on This Adventure

We invite you to support our mission through financial contributions. Your support will help us expand our research capabilities, enhance our training programs, and develop new technologies and initiatives that will shape the future of space medicine. Explore how you can contribute to our groundbreaking work through philanthropy. Together, we can pioneer the future of human spaceflight and ensure the health and safety of humanity as we venture beyond Earth.

![UCLA Health](/themes/custom/uclahealth/dist/images/ucla-health-logo-footer.svg)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Space medicine is a specialized field focusing on the physiological and biological effects of spaceflight on humans, ensuring astronaut health and performance in extreme environments.
Human factors are critical in space medicine as they address psychological and physiological challenges astronauts face, impacting mission success and crew well-being in isolation and confinement.
Space exploration poses unique challenges like microgravity effects, radiation exposure, and psychological stressors, necessitating advanced medical solutions to ensure astronaut safety and health.
